summary: McKenzie analyzes the competitive landscape for his first software product, Bingo Card Creator, a tool for teachers to generate printable bingo cards. He systematically evaluates existing solutions—free web tools, paid educational software packages, and physical bingo sets—identifying specific usability failures and missing features in each. His core argument is that a well-defined niche (teachers needing quick, customizable bingo card generation) is being underserved despite existing competitors. He positions his product by focusing on workflow efficiency: reducing clicks, saving paper, and letting teachers store word lists.
key_points: The top-ranking competitor requires 75 extra clicks to print a classroom set of cards, revealing how small UX failures create exploitable market gaps
Competing products either bundle bingo as a minor feature in expensive packages or offer free tools with crippling limitations, leaving a gap for a focused, affordable solution
Understanding the customer's actual workflow (paper budgets, font sizes for young students, reusable word lists) matters more than raw feature counts
Non-software alternatives like physical bingo sets prove market demand exists, but are inflexible and expensive per-use compared to software
A microISV's free trial is its most dangerous competitor, a topic he flags for the next post
money_quotes: I'm setting out to do one thing very well and intend to do so, but my product won't be right for everybody.
Printing 25 copies (a classroom worth) requires seventy five additional mouseclicks and 25 page loads. They can't offer an entire lesson in 5 minutes like I can.
Do you know how much money the average school spends on paper every year? I used to work for an office supply company. Our educational orders would boggle your mind.
This is quite possibly an excellent resource for a teacher seeking a package of instructional aids which includes a passable bingo creator, but isn't attractive just for bingo.
All uISVs with a free trial need to understand that they compete with themselves for their customers.
The fact that they do proves the existence of a market.
key_insight: A focused product that solves one specific workflow pain point better than bloated competitors or free tools with poor UX can carve out a viable market niche.
